data.title: ZWB:CA ETF: Decent Bank-Focused Covered Call ETF, But Not Beating Peers
data.description:
ZWB:CA At a Glance

As the market remains choppy, covered call ETFs have become a legit sleeve in my portfolio, and one that I'm keeping an eye on is the BMO Covered Call Canadian Banks ETF (ZWB:CA), which is a passively managed exchange-traded fund with a NAV of about $4 billion CAD that invests in North American bank companies while also writing covered call options on the underlying holdings to generate additional cash. BMO has designed ZWB:CA to appeal to income-focused investors who want the defensive characteristics of the Canadian banking sector but with a yield boost from options premiums on top of the base dividend income.

With the US and Israel continuing to strike Iran, oil prices have surged above $90, and the Strait of Hormuz has been effectively shut to tanker traffic. While equity markets have rallied in April on buzz that Trump thinks the war is over, in this case he's not the only stakeholder, with both Israel and Iran involved as more than just side participants. In this environment, I think defensiveness and income generation of ZWB:CA are a plus.

Meanwhile, the Canadian banking sector continues to chug along nicely. ZWB:CA gives you some protection with a managed, diversified, income-generating exposure to these and related names without being trapped in a single-name bank story, like Scotiabank's struggles a few years ago. Given the geopolitical outlook and the makeup of the fund, I like ZWB:CA as an idea and think it's worth having some lower-risk, cash-generating options, but I prefer HMAX:CA as a higher-yield sleeve, which has captured more total return upside the past two years. I'll rate it a Hold for now but recognize it's a solid choice.

Fund Breakdown

ZWB:CA was launched on January 28, 2011, by BMO Asset Management with a mandate to provide exposure to banks to generate income and long-term capital appreciation while mitigating downside risk through covered call options.

It's worth noting that the fund writes call options on its underlying holdings—generally with one to two months to expiry—and collects the option premium as additional income. The trade-off is clear, though, and noted in the annual report: ZWB:CA will tend to be solid in flat or down markets and underperform in sharp rallies, as the covered calls cap some of the upside on a portion of the portfolio. And right now, with market uncertainty elevated and implied volatility rising due to the Iran conflict, the premium income generated by the covered call overlay has been a nice touch.

From a liquidity standpoint, the fund trades about 436,000 units per day on the TSX, with an average bid-ask spread of just 0.05%—excellent for a Canadian-listed ETF. This is important because there are tons of illiquid funds on the TSX, which are thinly traded and prone to wider spreads and NAV mispricing. While the fund's expense ratio is 0.72%—slightly elevated—it is justified by the active options overlay and monthly distributions.

ZWB:CA vs. HMAX:CA

The fund is not the only player in the game, with the Hamilton Canadian Financials YIELD MAXIMIZER ETF (HMAX:CA) generating lots of headlines recently. It's a Hamilton-based covered call ETF, which is similar in theory but different in execution. Hamilton started as a boutique fund manager in 2009 but quickly grew to be an active covered call fund manager, drawing headlines with splashy yield expectations and unique sector coverage for covered calls. While I hold some Hamilton funds, ZWB:CA tries to take a different approach.

Both funds are built on the same underlying engine—Canadian financials—but take different approaches to generating income. ZWB:CA writes out-of-the-money covered calls, which allows for some continued stock appreciation while modestly enhancing yield. HMAX:CA, by contrast, writes more aggressive, closer-to-the-money calls to maximize option premiums, resulting in significantly higher income but more limited upside in strong markets. This distinction matters because covered call strategies tend to perform best in flat or choppy environments and lag in bull markets. Over time, this shows up in performance: ZWB:CA has generally delivered stronger total returns, while HMAX:CA's higher yield can make it look attractive in the short term—especially when bank stocks are already performing well—but that advantage is often cyclical rather than structural.

The better choice ultimately depends on your objective. ZWB:CA is more suitable if you care about total return, want exposure to bank earnings growth, and view income as a complement rather than the primary goal—essentially enhancing yield without sacrificing long-term compounding. HMAX:CA, on the other hand, is designed for investors prioritizing immediate cash flow, even at the expense of future growth. It fits better in a portfolio where the goal is to generate consistent income today, with less concern for capital appreciation or NAV growth over time. And that has proven out since both ETFs have been on the market, as noted below in the chart of about two years. While ZWB:CA has done better (expected given its out-of-the-money call strategy), the total return for the fund is similar, as HMAX:CA generates about 7.4% more yield per year.

Fund Portfolio and Risk Profile

As of March 31, 2026, ZWB:CA holds a portfolio that is concentrated in the big bank stocks, pretty well evenly. I think that this is a well-diversified basket: no single name is so overweight. But I prefer to be cap-weighted vs. equal-weighted. HMAX:CA has a cap weighting that sees Royal Bank of Canada (RY) with a higher weighting, which makes sense as a global institution versus here, where National Bank of Canada (NA:CA), a Canadian (and more so Quebec) focused institution, is 5x smaller than RBC. That said, all the names in ZWB:CA are solid, and the covered call approach is lower risk.

The yield is what the investors look for. ZWB:CA distributes monthly with an annualized yield of ~5.3% based on recent prices. This is a bit higher than what most individual bank stocks offer on their own, but not materially so.

From a risk perspective, ZWB:CA's beta of 0.89 (5-year monthly) is low; the fund has roughly half the volatility of the broad market, which is exactly what you'd expect from a covered call utility ETF. The primary risk I'd flag is the covered call cap on upside. In a big equity market rally, ZWB:CA will underperform a utility index ETF or the broader market, as the written calls limit participation in sharp gains. That said, for income-focused investors with a defensive mindset, this trade-off is usually acceptable.

On the macro side, the banks will follow the overall economy and how the world bounces back from the energy crisis. That said, the diversification across the 6 big banks shields it somewhat. Rate sensitivity is also worth noting—usually, banks tend to be rate-sensitive, and if the Bank of Canada reverses rate cuts sharply, that could create some headwinds for the sector.

Bottom Line

The bottom line for ZWB:CA, in my opinion, is that this is a solid income vehicle available to Canadian investors right now, but I think that I like HMAX:CA better. ZWB:CA offers a decent yield via monthly distributions, but the 0.71% expense ratio is not cheap, and investors are equally weighted across six banks that are different from a scale and geography perspective. If you want an income ETF with some downside protection amidst market chaos, I like ZWB:CA but prefer HMAX:CA, with its stronger yield generation and cap-weighted holdings (historically in Canada, bigger banks do better long term).

data.title: Flowserve: The Unconventional Energy Play That Pays Off
data.description:
After being excluded from the S&P 500 a couple of years ago, Flowserve (FLS) was unlikely to be considered as a solid investment opportunity by most market participants. On top of that, FLS is also a rather unconventional play to gain exposure to the energy sector, as its pumps and valves businesses give investors an indirect exposure to the oil & gas and nuclear sectors.

In spite of all that, FLS has outperformed the S&P 500 since mid-2021, when I showed why the recently excluded stock from the most popular index holds enormous potential.

On top of outperforming the S&P 500, FLS has also proven to be a solid performer within the energy sector, with the stock recently catching up to the stellar performance of State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE).

Although over the long run, Flowserve's share price moves in unison with oil prices, in recent years this relationship is less profound as we are entering into an investment cycle within the energy sector more broadly.

This is confirmed by FLS's growth in bookings by segment, where power generation experienced double-digit growth in the last quarter of 2025.

This shows Flowserve's structural importance within a number of industries, from general ones to energy, power generation, and chemical. Bookings by sector are also relatively well-diversified, which puts FLS in a very good spot to continue outperforming the broader equity market.

Investors should also be aware that FLS is scheduled to report its Q1 2026 earnings after the market closes tomorrow on the 29th of April. One of the key aspects to watch during the report would be any management commentary regarding the recent geopolitical events and if they would have any material impact on FLS's top or bottom-line figures.

While certain short-term risks could manifest in the upcoming report, investors should have their eyes firmly on the full-year guidance and the long-term margin target of Flowserve's management.

So far, FLS has achieved its 2027 margin target roughly 2 years ahead of schedule, and it remains to be seen whether the margin goalpost will be moved.

Record-high Profitability

Flowserve's topline grew by low single digits in the last quarter of 2025 as strength in the pumps division (FPD) was offset by revenue declines in the industrial valves segment (FCD). According to FLS's management, the latter was largely due to project delays, and they expect revenue growth to normalize in 2026.

More importantly, margins in both divisions improved in 2025, and they both stood at around 20% on an adjusted basis. On a GAAP basis, the FPD segment remained as the key driver of profitability, and the operating margin is now at a record high (see the graph below).

For the current fiscal year, Flowserve's management anticipates further margin improvement, which would continue to support the share price even in the face of the low organic growth of 1% to 3%. Additionally, the company's low net leverage (standing at 1.0x as of Q4 of 202%) would allow the company to engage in strategic acquisitions. The nearly $500 million transaction for Trillium Flow Technologies' Valves Division would bump FLS's total revenue growth to 5% to 7% and will be accretive for margins once synergies and economies of scale are realized.

Moreover, the aforementioned deal was made at an EBITDA multiple of just slightly above 12, which is below the multiple that FLS currently trades at (see the graph below). After accounting for any potential synergies, this multiple significantly increases the probability of the deal creating long-term value for shareholders.

Moderating Returns

While Flowserve's end markets would continue to support demand for its products, investors should not simply extrapolate returns from recent years into the future.

Even though margins are still on an upward trajectory and with them Flowserve's return on equity should also improve in 2026, the stock's current price-to-book multiple of almost 5 is approaching the cyclical highs from the 2012-14 period when oil & gas majors were investing heavily into capacity.

The stock does not stand out as being overvalued at the moment, and this is true on a free cash flow basis as well. The current free cash flow yield of 4% is roughly in line with the historical average.

Cash flow should improve materially in 2026 on the back of higher margins, but at the same time, Flowserve's capex has now fallen below the company's annual depreciation expense.

A ratio of capex to D&A expense of below 1 is usually unsustainable over the long run, especially for a business that is expected to grow. Thus, higher capital expenditure will likely put more pressure on free cash flow going forward, and investors should also be prepared for any potential short-term disruptions in demand from the current geopolitical events in the Middle East.

Conclusion

Flowserve has turned into yet another example of a major index exclusion becoming a high-performing stock. Given the company's unique positioning in key sectors and long-term demand drivers within the energy and power generation segments, FLS remains a solid long-term buy. Having said that, investors should moderate their return expectations, as the stock is now priced near cyclical highs.

data.title: Carnival's Core Business Is Cruising - Initiating With A Buy
data.description:
While my analysis is mostly focused on the tech sector, the travel sector has potentially become quite interesting as the Iran war has caused massive volatility in energy prices. For a cruise company like Carnival Corporation & plc (CCL), fuel costs are a significant influence on their business. As you can see in the chart below, while 52-week gains are still at a very respectable 45%, the stock has dipped considerably from the highs seen earlier this year. The company reported their earnings around a month ago but won't report again until late June, and so today I have decided to initiate coverage.

Below, it is shown that Q1 was an impressive quarter for Carnival. Demand is rock-solid, and strong net yield growth shows that core profitability is in a good position. Moving forward, fuel costs are obviously the number one risk to their business, but with some operational mitigative efforts in place and the long-term direction of the company unchanged, I would say investors should avoid overreacting to energy volatility-induced stock movements. With capital returns being tempting and the valuation having pulled back recently, I have decided to initiate Carnival at a buy rating since their core business is cruising right now.

Demand Is Robust

At the end of the day, the strength of demand for their cruises is one of the most important signals of their business health, and so we'll start this discussion with a look at their deposit numbers and their bookings as well. As you can see on the right, Carnival had record customer deposits in their Q1, as there was 10% growth YoY to show that the company is taking in a significant amount of cash. For 2026 sailings, bookings saw double-digit increases, and they are also seeing strong interest for sailings in the next calendar year and beyond. At this point, I would say that it is quite clear that the demand for their cruises is robust. With consumer confidence not being particularly strong in many key economies in the world, I would say that their deposit and bookings performance speaks volumes regarding their value proposition.

Profitability Looks Solid

In terms of the financial performance of the business, there are strong indications that Carnival is in a solid position. Firstly, for the top line, they saw record revenues of $6.2 billion, up ~6.9% YoY. This beat analyst expectations by a fair margin of $26.46 million to show some outperformance.

For profitability, the company also reported impressive numbers. Net yield (in constant currency) is, of course, a key metric, and for Q1, they saw a 2.7% YoY increase to a record high as well. This shows that the core profitability is exceptionally solid right now. For adjusted cruise costs excl. fuel per ALBD, there was a 5.3% increase. Since that is notably lower than the top-line growth rate, this is another sign that profitability is in a good position.

Carnival also delivered on their commitment to use less fuel, as there was a 4.7% drop in consumption per ALBD in Q1. This will be important for obvious reasons given the geopolitical environment right now. Lastly, for the bottom line, adjusted EPS of $0.20 represented a 50% jump YoY and beat expectations by $0.02. With revenues being strong and costs well controlled, financial performance in Q1 was very respectable in my view.

Capital Returns Are Tempting

We will take a look at their financial guidance next, but one of the potentially most encouraging signs for investors is the company's willingness to return capital. From 2026 to 2029, they are targeting around $14 billion of shareholder distributions. For a company that has a market cap of $37.55 billion as of this writing, this shows that capital returns will be very generous moving forward.

In the nearer term, Carnival expects to pay out more than $800 million in dividends in 2026, and they announced a $2.5 billion share repurchase program on the day their Q1 earnings came out. To provide further confidence that the company has the means to distribute this amount of capital, they stated that the dividend payout and stock buybacks will happen "all while further strengthening the balance sheet." Therefore, for investors that are seeking income or capital returns in general, Carnival presents a tempting proposition.

Financial Guidance

Carnival's quarterly and annual adjusted EBITDA and adjusted EPS guidance reportedly missed consensus expectations amid geopolitical tensions. In this section, let's take a look at their full year 2026 expectations for a better sense of where things are headed. As stated earlier, a key metric for the company is net yield, and as you can see above, they are expecting to see constant currency growth of 2.75%. Therefore, robust core profitability is expected moving forward.

While total fuel consumption is expected to be basically unchanged from 2025 levels, note that fuel cost per metric ton consumed (excluding emission allowances) is unsurprisingly going to be higher than in the previous year. In 2025, this metric was at $610, and so there will be a considerable jump to $718. This is obviously the key risk that is affecting Carnival's profitability. For the bottom line, their $2.21 estimate for adjusted EPS implies an around 1.8% annual decline, and so it is clear that fuel costs are going to take a big bite out of the company's profits.

Risk Mitigation and Long-Term Direction

While fuel cost volatility driven by the Iran war is a major risk to Carnival's profitability, the company is taking steps to try to mitigate this the best they can. In their earnings release, they stated that they are expecting almost $150 million of operational improvement for full-year adjusted net income versus December guidance. While the updated guidance displayed above shows that a complete offset of the fuel cost headwinds won't happen, investors should definitely give Carnival some credit. Instead of being static, they are actively finding ways to soften the blow, and so I would say the company is one that's effectively managed.

When taking a long-term look at Carnival's business, investors have quite a few reasons to be encouraged, in my view. A highlight of their Q1 earnings is the fact that they announced their PROPEL targets. As you can see above, these are initiatives that should ultimately benefit shareholders. For example, yield expansion is of course key for profitability, and over the PROPEL period, they are only expecting 3 ship deliveries. This shows that they are being prudent with regard to capacity expansion. While fuel costs are driving big moves in the stock right now, I believe it is important to view these headwinds relative to the big picture and where the company is going in the long run.

Valuation Analysis

After the recent pullback and subsequent partial rebound, Carnival's forward P/E stands at a reading of 12.0. Seeking Alpha's valuation page for the stock shows that this multiple is at a more than 20% discount to the Consumer Discretionary sector as of this writing, and so I believe there is a decent amount of opportunity in the stock right now for investors.

With the exception of the outlook for fuel costs, Carnival's Q1 was shown to be robust in today's analysis. Demand is rock solid as deposits and bookings rise significantly, and a rising net yield shows that core profitability is strong. Furthermore, the announcement of a new share buyback program and long-term capital return targets should be music to investors' ears. While fuel costs are a major driver of stock movements right now, I would say that it would be a mistake for long-term investors to be deterred by this. As discussed above, the company has near-term mitigation strategies in place, and the long-term direction is unchanged by these temporary headwinds.

With that being the case, I would say that the forward P/E's recent pullback from 2026 highs and the discount to the Consumer Discretionary sector show that the risk/reward for Carnival stock is currently favorable for patient investors.

Conclusion

While the market is focused on every energy headline that shows up, in reality Carnival's core business is cruising below the surface. Short-termism, like overreacting to temporary fuel cost headwinds, is usually a good way to erode long-term wealth, and so, in my view, investors should really focus on the key metrics like net yield and also on the long-term strategy of the business. When taking this perspective, it is quite clear that the recent pullback in the valuation and the discount at which the stock is trading at relative to the sector is a buying opportunity for patient investors. Therefore, I have decided to initiate Carnival with a bullish call.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
